terms.definition: A decimal number equal to one thousandth, written with three digits after the decimal point.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 0.001 is one thousandth — you need one thousand of them to make 1.
terms.common_errors: Confusing 0.001 with 0.01 or 0.1 — each additional zero makes the value ten times smaller.

terms.definition: A key mathematical fact: the number 1 has only one factor (itself), so it does not meet the definition of a prime number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Remember, 1 is not prime because prime numbers must have exactly two factors.
terms.common_errors: Assuming 1 is prime because it is only divisible by itself.

terms.definition: A fraction representing one part out of one thousand equal parts.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: One millimetre is 1/1000 of a metre.
terms.common_errors: Thinking 1/1000 is larger than 1/100 because 1000 is a bigger number.

terms.definition: A mental calculation strategy where you round a number to make a calculation easier, then compensate for the rounding.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: To add 99, try adjusting: add 100 then subtract 1.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to compensate after adjusting — adding 100 but not subtracting the 1.

terms.definition: The angles around a single point that together make a complete turn of 360°.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The angles at a point always add up to 360 degrees.
terms.common_errors: Confusing angles at a point (360°) with angles on a straight line (180°).

terms.definition: The three interior angles of any triangle, which always sum to exactly 180°.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If two angles in a triangle are 60° and 80°, the third must be 40° because angles in a triangle add up to 180°.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting that this rule applies to all triangles, not just equilateral ones.

terms.definition: Two or more angles that share a straight line as their base and together sum to exactly 180°.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If one angle on a straight line is 120°, the other must be 60°.
terms.common_errors: Adding angles to 360° instead of 180° — confusing with angles at a point.

terms.definition: One of the two straight lines that form an angle, meeting at the vertex.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: An angle is made where two arms meet at a point.

terms.definition: A horizontal reference line used as the starting edge for measuring angles with a protractor.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Line up the baseline of the protractor with one arm of the angle.
terms.common_errors: Not aligning the baseline correctly, leading to inaccurate angle measurements.

terms.definition: An informal name for the short division algorithm, where the dividend sits inside the division bracket.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.etymology: Named because the layout resembles a bus stop shelter
terms.example_usage: Use the bus stop method to divide 432 by 6.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to carry remainders to the next column.

terms.definition: Arranged in columns according to place value; used to describe the standard written layout for addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Set out your subtraction in columnar form with the digits lined up.
terms.common_errors: Not aligning digits in the correct columns, leading to place-value errors.

terms.definition: A shared denominator that two or more fractions can be converted to, enabling them to be added, subtracted, or compared.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: To add 1/3 and 1/4, find a common denominator: 12.
terms.common_errors: Only changing the denominator without adjusting the numerator to keep the fraction equivalent.

terms.definition: A number that divides exactly into two or more other numbers with no remainder.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The common factors of 12 and 18 are 1, 2, 3, and 6.
terms.common_errors: Confusing common factors with common multiples.

terms.definition: A number that appears in the times tables of two or more numbers.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The common multiples of 3 and 4 include 12, 24, and 36.
terms.common_errors: Confusing common multiples with common factors.

terms.definition: Examining two or more numbers, quantities, or measures to determine which is greater, smaller, or whether they are equal.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Make a comparison: which fraction is larger, 3/5 or 2/3?

terms.definition: A whole number greater than 1 that has more than two factors; the opposite of a prime number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 12 is a composite number because it has factors 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, and 12.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting that 4 is the smallest composite number, not 2.

terms.definition: Exactly the same shape and size; two shapes are congruent if one can be placed exactly on top of the other.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: These two triangles are congruent — they are identical in shape and size.
terms.common_errors: Confusing congruent (same shape and size) with similar (same shape, different size).

terms.definition: The real-life situation or scenario in which a mathematical problem is set, helping children see the purpose of the calculation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In the context of a recipe, we need to multiply the ingredients by 3.

terms.definition: The result of multiplying a whole number by itself three times (e.g. 2 × 2 × 2 = 8).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 8 is a cube number because 2 × 2 × 2 = 8.
terms.common_errors: Confusing cube numbers with square numbers — cubed means three multiplications, squared means two.

terms.definition: Raised to the power of three; multiplied by itself twice (n × n × n), written as n³.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 3 cubed is 27 because 3 × 3 × 3 = 27.
terms.common_errors: Computing n × 3 instead of n × n × n.

terms.definition: A unit of volume equal to a cube with edges of 1 cm, written as cm³.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This box has a volume of 24 cubic centimetres.
terms.common_errors: Confusing cm³ (volume) with cm² (area) or cm (length).

terms.definition: How precisely a number has been rounded, stated as the nearest whole number, ten, hundred, or decimal place.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Round 3.456 to one decimal place — the degree of accuracy is one decimal place.
terms.common_errors: Rounding to the wrong place value when the degree of accuracy is specified.

terms.definition: The unit of measurement for angles, represented by the symbol °; a full turn is 360°.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This angle measures 45 degrees.
terms.common_errors: Confusing degrees for temperature with degrees for angles.

terms.definition: The number being divided in a division calculation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 432 ÷ 6, the dividend is 432.
terms.common_errors: Confusing dividend (number being divided) with divisor (number you divide by).

terms.definition: A number that can be divided by another number with no remainder.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 15 is divisible by 3 because 15 ÷ 3 = 5 with no remainder.
terms.common_errors: Saying a number is divisible by itself as a test — every number is divisible by itself.

terms.definition: The number you divide by in a division calculation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 432 ÷ 6, the divisor is 6.
terms.common_errors: Confusing divisor with dividend.

terms.definition: A fraction that represents the same value as another fraction, even though the numerator and denominator are different.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 2/4 is an equivalent fraction to 1/2.
terms.common_errors: Adding the same number to numerator and denominator instead of multiplying by the same factor.

terms.definition: To calculate a given proportion of a quantity by converting the percentage to a fraction or decimal and multiplying.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find 10% of 350 by dividing by 10: the answer is 35.
terms.common_errors: Dividing by the percentage number instead of converting it first.

terms.definition: The four sections of a coordinate grid created by the x-axis and y-axis, including areas with negative coordinates.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In Year 6, we plot coordinates in all four quadrants, including negative values.
terms.common_errors: Not understanding that coordinates can be negative in some quadrants.

terms.definition: An abbreviation for Highest Common Factor — the largest number that divides exactly into two or more given numbers.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The HCF of 12 and 18 is 6.
terms.common_errors: Confusing HCF with LCM — HCF is the largest shared factor, LCM is the smallest shared multiple.

terms.definition: The largest whole number that divides exactly into two or more given numbers with no remainder.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The highest common factor of 24 and 36 is 12.
terms.common_errors: Listing factors incorrectly or stopping before finding the highest one.

terms.definition: The number 100,000; ten groups of ten thousand or one tenth of a million.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: One hundred thousand people live in this town.
terms.common_errors: Difficulty with the number of zeros — 100,000 has five zeros.

terms.definition: The new position of a shape after a transformation such as reflection, rotation, or translation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: After reflecting the shape, label the image A'B'C'.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the image (result) with the object (original shape).

terms.definition: A fraction where the numerator is greater than or equal to the denominator, representing a value of one or more.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 7/4 is an improper fraction — it is more than one whole.
terms.common_errors: Thinking improper fractions are wrong or incorrect — they are a valid way to express values greater than 1.

terms.definition: A notch or small cut into the edge of a shape, creating an interior angle greater than 180° (a reflex angle).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This polygon has an indent that makes it concave.

terms.definition: An abbreviation for Lowest Common Multiple — the smallest number that appears in the times tables of two or more given numbers.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The LCM of 4 and 6 is 12.
terms.common_errors: Confusing LCM with HCF.

terms.definition: A written method for multiplying by a two-digit or larger number, using partial products that are then added together.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use long multiplication to calculate 234 × 56.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to use a placeholder zero when multiplying by the tens digit.

terms.definition: The smallest whole number that is a multiple of two or more given numbers.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The lowest common multiple of 6 and 8 is 24.
terms.common_errors: Multiplying the two numbers together when a smaller common multiple exists.

terms.definition: A method for solving a calculation in your head, using known facts and number relationships.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use a mental strategy to calculate 56 + 29: add 30 and subtract 1.

terms.definition: The number 1,000,000; one thousand groups of one thousand.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are one million centimetres in 10 kilometres.
terms.common_errors: Not appreciating the scale — a million is a thousand thousands.

terms.definition: An unknown angle that can be calculated using known angle facts (e.g. angles in a triangle sum to 180°).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the missing angle in this triangle if two angles are 70° and 55°.
terms.common_errors: Using the wrong angle rule — e.g. applying the triangle rule to angles on a straight line.

terms.definition: A number made up of a whole number and a proper fraction combined (e.g. 2¾).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Convert 11/4 to a mixed number: 2 and 3/4.
terms.common_errors: When converting, dividing incorrectly or losing the remainder.

terms.definition: A word problem that requires two or more separate calculations to reach the final answer.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This is a multi-step problem: first find the cost of 3 items, then calculate the change from £10.
terms.common_errors: Only completing the first step and giving that as the final answer.

terms.definition: The notation for a number squared — multiplied by itself once — written with a small 2 above the number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 5² means 5 squared, which is 5 × 5 = 25.
terms.common_errors: Computing n × 2 instead of n × n.

terms.definition: The original shape before a transformation is applied; the starting position.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Label the object shape before you draw its reflection.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the object (original) with the image (result after transformation).

terms.definition: In mathematics, used to indicate multiplication when finding a fraction or percentage of a quantity.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find 3/4 of 20 means calculate 3/4 × 20.
terms.common_errors: Not recognising that 'of' means multiply in fraction contexts.

terms.definition: A mathematical process: addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Which operation do you need to solve this problem — addition or multiplication?
terms.common_errors: Choosing the wrong operation for a word problem.

terms.definition: Breaking a number into parts based on place value or other useful groupings to make calculations easier.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Partition 347 into 300 + 40 + 7 to help with addition.
terms.common_errors: Only partitioning by place value when a different split would be more useful.

terms.definition: For every hundred; the literal meaning of percent, describing a proportion out of 100.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 25 per hundred means 25 out of every 100, or 25%.

terms.definition: A proportion expressed as a number out of 100, represented by the symbol %.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 50 percent means 50 out of 100, which is the same as one half.
terms.common_errors: Not connecting percent to hundredths — 1% = 1/100 = 0.01.

terms.definition: A way of expressing a number as a fraction of 100, used to compare proportions.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What percentage of the class chose football? 15 out of 30 is 50%.
terms.common_errors: Treating percentages as fixed amounts rather than proportions.

terms.definition: The shortest distance from a point to a line, measured at a right angle (90°) to the line.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The height of a triangle is the perpendicular distance from the base to the top vertex.
terms.common_errors: Measuring the slanted distance instead of the perpendicular (shortest) distance.

terms.definition: A zero used to hold a place-value position, ensuring digits are in the correct column.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 3,025, the zero is a placeholder in the hundreds column.
terms.common_errors: Omitting placeholder zeros when writing numbers, e.g. writing 325 instead of 3,025.

terms.definition: A way of expressing repeated multiplication; the exponent tells you how many times to multiply the base by itself.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 2 to the power of 3 means 2 × 2 × 2 = 8.
terms.common_errors: Confusing powers with multiplication — 2³ is 8, not 6.

terms.definition: A number formed by multiplying 10 by itself a given number of times: 10, 100, 1000, 10000, etc.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 1,000 is 10 to the power of 3, which is 10 × 10 × 10.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the exponent with the number of zeros — 10³ has 3 zeros (1,000).

terms.definition: A whole number greater than 1 that has exactly two factors: 1 and itself.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 7 is a prime number because its only factors are 1 and 7.
terms.common_errors: Including 1 as a prime number — it only has one factor, not two.

terms.definition: A semicircular measuring instrument marked in degrees, used to measure and draw angles.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use a protractor to measure this angle — it is 72 degrees.
terms.common_errors: Reading from the wrong scale (inner vs outer) on a dual-scale protractor.

terms.definition: An angle that measures more than 180° but less than 360°.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The reflex angle at this corner is 270 degrees.
terms.common_errors: Measuring the acute or obtuse angle instead of the reflex angle at a vertex.

terms.definition: Approximating a number to a nearby simpler value based on place value, using the rule that 5 or above rounds up.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Rounding 3,467 to the nearest hundred gives 3,500.
terms.common_errors: Rounding each digit sequentially instead of looking only at the relevant deciding digit.

terms.definition: A horizontal line of items, numbers, or cells in a table or array, running left to right.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Read across the row to find the data for March.
terms.common_errors: Confusing rows (horizontal) with columns (vertical).

terms.definition: A compact written method for dividing a multi-digit number by a single-digit divisor, carrying remainders mentally.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use short division to calculate 738 ÷ 6.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to carry the remainder to the next digit.

terms.definition: A systematic method for finding all prime numbers up to a given value by repeatedly crossing out multiples.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.etymology: Named after the Greek mathematician Eratosthenes (c. 276-194 BC)
terms.example_usage: Use the Sieve of Eratosthenes to find all primes up to 100.
terms.common_errors: Crossing out the prime number itself instead of just its multiples.

terms.definition: The three-dimensional extent in which objects exist; in maths, used when discussing volume, capacity, and 3D shapes.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: How much space does this box take up?

terms.definition: The result of multiplying a whole number by itself (e.g. 4 × 4 = 16); can be represented as a square array of dots.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 25 is a square number because 5 × 5 = 25.
terms.common_errors: Confusing square numbers with even numbers — not all square numbers are even (e.g. 9, 25).

terms.definition: Multiplied by itself once; raised to the power of 2, written as n².
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 6 squared is 36 because 6 × 6 = 36.
terms.common_errors: Computing n × 2 instead of n × n — 6 squared is 36, not 12.

terms.definition: An angle measuring exactly 180°, forming a straight line.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A straight angle looks like a flat line and measures 180 degrees.
terms.common_errors: Not recognising 180° as an angle because it looks like a straight line.

terms.definition: Two angles that add together to make exactly 180° (a straight line).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 70° and 110° are supplementary angles because they add up to 180°.
terms.common_errors: Confusing supplementary (180°) with complementary (90°).

terms.definition: The number 10,000; ten groups of one thousand.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Ten thousand has four zeros: 10,000.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with one thousand — miscounting the zeros.

terms.definition: One of one thousand equal parts of a whole; the third digit after the decimal point represents thousandths.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 0.125, the 5 is in the thousandths place.
terms.common_errors: Confusing thousandths with thousands.

terms.definition: A level of precision showing three digits after the decimal point (tenths, hundredths, thousandths).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Round 4.5678 to three decimal places: 4.568.
terms.common_errors: Counting the digits before the decimal point as decimal places.

terms.definition: Having length, width, and height (depth); occupying physical space rather than being flat.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A cube is a three-dimensional shape with 6 square faces.
terms.common_errors: Confusing 3D shapes with their 2D faces — a cube is 3D, a square is 2D.

terms.definition: A table showing scheduled times for events or transport; used in maths for reading and interpreting time-based data.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the bus timetable to work out how long the journey takes.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the 12-hour and 24-hour clock formats when reading timetables.

terms.definition: A change in the position, size, or orientation of a shape — includes reflection, rotation, and translation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Describe the transformation that moves shape A to shape B.
terms.common_errors: Not specifying the type of transformation (reflection, rotation, or translation).

terms.definition: A transformation that slides a shape to a new position without rotating or flipping it; every point moves the same distance in the same direction.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Translate the shape 3 squares right and 2 squares up.
terms.common_errors: Accidentally rotating or reflecting the shape during translation.

terms.definition: A number between 10 and 99 used as the multiplier in a multiplication calculation, requiring the long multiplication method.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use long multiplication when you have a two-digit multiplier like 34.

terms.definition: A cube with edges of exactly 1 unit length, used as the standard building block for measuring volume.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count how many unit cubes fit inside the box to find its volume.

terms.definition: A number without fractions or decimals; the counting numbers (0, 1, 2, 3, ...) including zero.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 7 is a whole number but 7.5 is not.
terms.common_errors: Debating whether zero is a whole number — it is.

terms.definition: Written or spoken number names, as opposed to digits or symbols; children learn to read and write numbers in words.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write 456 in words: four hundred and fifty-six.
terms.common_errors: Omitting 'and' in number words — UK convention includes it (e.g. one hundred and two).
